aspects.name: Punctuation between clauses
aspects.description: The student must choose punctuation at the boundary where two independent clauses, or an independent and a dependent clause, meet. The cognitive operation is identifying whether each side of the blank is a complete sentence and applying the correct joiner: period, semicolon, comma+coordinating conjunction, comma+subordinator, or no punctuation.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 governs the junction between full clauses; A2 governs boundaries around inserted non-essential material within one clause; turning a comma-splice/run-on test into an appositive-set-off test is a categorical drift.

types.name: Independent + Independent join
types.description: Two independent clauses flank the blank with no conjunction. Correct answer is a period (new sentence) or a semicolon; distractors include comma alone (splice), no punctuation (run-on), and comma+subordinator.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that both sides of the blank are complete clauses with no conjunction supplied, then select either a period or semicolon and reject comma-only or unpunctuated options.

characteristics: Both clauses are short and syntactically simple
Period vs semicolon is the only correct form; three distractors are obvious errors (no punct, lone comma, 'and' tacked on)
Capitalization of the next word cues sentence boundary
easy.example_stem: ...weather conditions three to four weeks in ______ its predictions are therefore more short-term...

characteristics: One or both clauses contain an intervening prepositional or participial phrase before the main verb
Distractors include a plausible comma+coordinator that doesn't match meaning or a semicolon misused before a fragment
Adjacent transition words (Prior to, According to, In a ___) may obscure clause boundary
medium.example_stem: ...updating all calculations dependent on these ______ to VisiCalc's release, changing a paper spreadsheet often required redoing the entire sheet...

characteristics: Second clause begins with a participial or prepositional phrase that resembles a fragment, increasing risk of choosing comma
All four options differ only in punctuation/capitalization micro-choices (e.g., 'plant. By' vs 'plant, by' vs 'plant) by')
Subject of second clause may be far from the boundary
hard.example_stem: ...inside a host ______ researching 120 endophytic fungi–produced compounds, she found...

must_preserve: Both sides of the blank must be syntactically independent clauses
No coordinating conjunction may appear in the stimulus around the blank
Correct answer must be period (new sentence) or semicolon
must_vary: Topic domain (science, arts, history, biography)
Length and internal complexity of each clause
Whether period or semicolon is the keyed answer
common_bugs: Cloner inserts a coordinating conjunction in the second clause, converting T1 into T2
Cloner shortens second clause to a fragment, accidentally enabling comma+appositive answer (A2 drift)
Cloner duplicates the original's exact joiner choices in distractors, breaking distractor parallelism

types.name: Comma + coordinating conjunction join
types.description: Two independent clauses are joined by a coordinating conjunction (and/but/so/yet/for/or) that already appears in the stem or option, and the student must place a comma before the conjunction (or recognize no comma before 'and' when the subject is shared).
types.core_skill_demand: Identify that a coordinating conjunction will join two independent clauses and place a comma before it; or recognize that the second side lacks its own subject and reject the comma.

characteristics: Both clauses are short and have explicit different subjects
Coordinating conjunction is overt in two of four options
Distractors include no comma, lone comma without 'but/and', and 'and' without comma
easy.example_stem: ...are more durable than most natural plant ______ the manufacture of synthetic fibers requires toxic chemical solvents...

characteristics: Distractors include semicolon+conjunction or comma-only without conjunction
Second clause's subject is a pronoun referring back, making clause-vs-phrase boundary subtler
medium.example_stem: Po'Pay... was instrumental in organizing the Pueblo Revolt of ______ as a result of his leadership, the Spanish colonizers were expelled...

characteristics: Stem also contains a parenthetical or modifier that could be mistaken as the clause boundary
Choice of 'and' vs no conjunction hinges on whether the second predicate shares the first clause's subject
Options differ in placement of comma vs presence of 'and' across four near-identical strings
hard.example_stem: ...founded in 1931 with the goal of showcasing the culture and history of the Mohegan ______ today, nearly a century later, it is the oldest Native-owned and -operated museum...

must_preserve: Exactly one coordinating conjunction available adjacent to the blank
Both clauses must have explicit, distinct grammatical subjects
Correct answer pairs a comma with the conjunction
must_vary: Choice of coordinating conjunction (and/but/so/yet)
Topic, named entities, dates
Whether the second clause begins with a transitional adverb
common_bugs: Cloner uses 'and' to join compound predicate (shared subject), making the comma+and answer incorrect
Cloner drops the explicit second subject, demoting to T3 with a dependent clause
Cloner picks both 'and' and 'but' in distractors without keeping the meaning relation consistent with the stem

types.name: Independent + dependent / subordinate join
types.description: An independent clause is followed (or preceded) by a dependent clause, relative clause, or subordinated phrase. The student must select the appropriate comma (often none, sometimes comma+relative/subordinator) and reject semicolon/period that would create a fragment.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that one side of the blank is a dependent or restrictive element and select punctuation that ties it to the main clause without creating a fragment; reject period or semicolon that would orphan the dependent piece.

characteristics: Relative clause beginning with 'which', 'who', 'that' immediately follows the blank
Correct answer is comma (nonrestrictive) or no punctuation (restrictive 'that')
Distractors include semicolon and period that obviously break the relative
easy.example_stem: ...with the passage of the Engravers' Copyright ______ gave engravers control over the distribution and sale...

characteristics: Subordinator ('while', 'whenever', 'because', 'so that') begins the second clause
Distractors offer plausible semicolon or comma-and-subordinator combos
Correct answer omits or places comma based on restrictive/nonrestrictive contrast
medium.example_stem: ...for instance, the colorful calico rockfish... can survive for a little over a ______ the rougheye rockfish... boasts a maximum life span of about two centuries.

characteristics: Dependent element is participial phrase or absolute construction that mimics a clause
Conjunctive adverb ('however', 'though', 'rather') sits at the join and is itself surrounded by punctuation choices
Correct answer often a semicolon+comma combination (e.g., '; however,' or ', though;')
hard.example_stem: Scott-Heron himself resisted the godfather ______ feeling that it didn't encapsulate his devotion...

must_preserve: One side of blank must be a dependent/subordinate/relative element, not a free-standing clause
Correct answer must avoid period/semicolon when keyed answer is comma or no punctuation
If a conjunctive adverb is present, its function as transition (not coordinator) must be preserved
must_vary: The subordinator or relative pronoun chosen
Whether the relative clause is restrictive or nonrestrictive
Position of the dependent element (before or after main clause)
common_bugs: Cloner converts 'that' to 'which' or vice versa, flipping restrictive/nonrestrictive and breaking the key
Cloner drops the subordinator, accidentally creating two independent clauses (T1 drift)
Cloner mismatches conjunctive-adverb punctuation: uses comma where semicolon required because second side is independent

aspects.name: Supplementary elements within a clause
aspects.description: The student must punctuate a non-essential insertion inside a clause: an appositive, parenthetical phrase, nonrestrictive modifier, or interrupter. The cognitive operation is identifying the boundaries of the inserted element and choosing matched commas, paired dashes, or parentheses to set it off symmetrically.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 inserts material into a single clause without breaking it; A1 joins two clauses. Treating an appositive as a clause break (or vice versa) is a categorical drift.

types.name: Set off an appositive or parenthetical
types.description: A descriptive phrase renaming or elaborating a noun is inserted before/after the blank. The student selects matched punctuation (paired commas, paired dashes, or paired parentheses) to enclose the insertion symmetrically.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the left and right edges of the supplementary phrase and choose the punctuation that matches at both edges; reject options with mismatched openers and closers.

characteristics: Insertion is a short noun phrase (one to three words) appositive
One edge of the insertion is already given in the stem; only the second edge varies
Distractors include no punctuation, comma+'and', and lone semicolon
easy.example_stem: While filming a boxing match for the movie Body and Soul ______ Howe had a handheld camera operator wear roller skates.

characteristics: Insertion includes its own embedded comma list, requiring outer dashes or parens
One given edge is a dash or parenthesis, forcing the matching mark on the other side
Distractors mix dash/paren/comma to expose mismatch-detection
medium.example_stem: Teotihuacan housed its residents (as many as 200,000, by some ______ in a complex of comfortable apartments...

characteristics: Insertion is a long descriptive clause containing its own commas
Stimulus contains multiple potential insertion edges, forcing identification of the actual span
Correct answer requires paired dashes around the entire span, with all comma-based options being incorrect
hard.example_stem: ...have a special genetic ability... that ______ ocean dwellers that include the squid, the octopus, and the cuttlefish ______ confers evolutionary advantages.

must_preserve: The blank must enclose a supplementary, non-essential element
The opposite edge of the insertion (whether a dash, paren, or comma) must remain fixed in the stem
Removing the insertion must leave a grammatical sentence
must_vary: Length and internal punctuation of the supplementary phrase
Choice of paired marks (commas vs dashes vs parentheses)
Position of insertion (mid-clause vs at clause edge)
common_bugs: Cloner flips the fixed opposite edge to a different mark, making no option produce a valid pair
Cloner inserts a verb into the supplementary phrase, accidentally turning it into a clause (A1 drift)
Cloner forgets that internal commas in the insertion force outer dashes/parens

types.name: Nonrestrictive vs restrictive modifier punctuation
types.description: A modifying phrase (often beginning with 'which', 'a [noun]', participial) sits at the boundary of a noun, and the student must decide whether to set it off with comma/dash (nonrestrictive) or leave unpunctuated (restrictive).
types.core_skill_demand: Determine whether the modifier supplies essential identifying information (no commas) or supplementary information (set off with commas/dashes); reject options that confuse the two patterns.

characteristics: Modifier is unambiguously restrictive: removing it changes meaning
Correct answer is no punctuation; distractors include comma, dash, colon
easy.example_stem: The element carbon has the highest melting point ______ all the elements on the periodic table—3,500 degrees Celsius.

characteristics: Modifier could be read either way; rationale relies on whether antecedent is fully specified
Distractor set includes both 'comma only' and 'no punctuation' to force the contrast
medium.example_stem: ...artists Isabel and Ruben Toledo redesigned the costumes and sets for The Miami City Ballet's production of The ______ to reviewers, the Toledos' designs helped infuse...

characteristics: Modifier follows a proper noun and itself contains internal punctuation
Choice between comma+appositive vs no punctuation hinges on definite-article cue ('a [noun]' vs '[noun]')
Stimulus contains two potential antecedents at different distances
hard.example_stem: ...Ray released his first movie, Pather ______ quiet black-and-white drama about a family in rural India, Ray's film was quite different...

must_preserve: The relationship between the noun and modifier must remain restrictive or nonrestrictive as keyed
Antecedent specificity (definite/indefinite, proper/common) must be preserved
must_vary: The modifier's surface form (participial, appositive, prepositional)
The proper noun referenced
common_bugs: Cloner adds 'the' or 'a' in front of the modifier and inadvertently flips restrictive to nonrestrictive
Cloner uses a different antecedent that already implies uniqueness, breaking the restrictive justification
Cloner introduces an extra comma elsewhere that creates a false parallel insertion

aspects.name: Titles, names, and noun-noun appositive pairs
aspects.description: The student must choose punctuation around a proper-noun title-and-name pair (e.g., 'linguist Diane Massam' vs 'linguist, Diane Massam,'). The cognitive operation is determining whether the name is restrictive (the title alone is insufficient identification) or nonrestrictive (the title already uniquely identifies the person/thing).
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 is a specialized appositive case where the test is exclusively about job-title-plus-name (or category-plus-instance) pairs; A2's broader appositive type covers all other inserted material. A3 questions universally feature a person's profession or role followed by a proper noun.

types.name: Role/title + proper noun (restrictive name)
types.description: A common-noun role (linguist, professor, sociologist, sculptor, biologist) is immediately followed by a proper name. The student must decide that the name is restrictive (no commas) because the role alone does not uniquely identify the individual.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that an indefinite role identifier requires the proper name as essential identification, and therefore reject all comma-bracketed options.

characteristics: Role is a generic professional title with one proper name following
Correct answer is no punctuation; only one distractor is plausible
easy.example_stem: ...chemistry ______ and his colleagues at Temple University recently developed a new approach...

characteristics: Stem includes a modifier before the role (pioneering, plant cell, materials scientist)
Distractors include comma-before-only and comma-around to test partial-application errors
medium.example_stem: ...Critics have noted the way pioneering ______ compares the experiences of issei and nisei.

characteristics: Two-of-four distractors set off the name with commas; one wraps in 'a [role]'
Stimulus mentions only one instance, so role-uniqueness analysis is non-obvious
Multiple roles named earlier in the paragraph could imply set membership
hard.example_stem: ...quotes critic ______ claims [the film] 'expanded the range of possibilities for Asian images on screen.'

must_preserve: Role term must be indefinite (no 'the', not preceded by previous unique mention)
Exactly one proper name follows the role
Correct answer must be no punctuation between role and name
must_vary: Profession or role used
Length of any modifying adjective before the role
Topic (sciences, arts, law)
common_bugs: Cloner adds 'the' before the role, making nonrestrictive valid and breaking the key
Cloner introduces a second name later in the paragraph that overlaps the role, undermining restrictiveness
Cloner uses a unique role ('the fourteenth US librarian of Congress') where commas would be correct (T7 drift)

types.name: Unique title/work + name (nonrestrictive)
types.description: A definite, uniquely-identifying noun phrase (e.g., 'his autobiography', 'the painting', 'her first novel') precedes a specific name; the name is a nonrestrictive appositive and must be set off with commas (or matched dashes).
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that the descriptor already uniquely picks out the referent, so the name is supplementary and must be set off with paired commas or dashes.

characteristics: Definite article or possessive precedes the descriptor
Correct answer is a single comma before the name (with sentence end after) or comma-name-comma
easy.example_stem: The short story 'Rogue Enchantments' by Isabel Ibañez appears in Reclaim the ______ anthology of fantasy and science fiction...

characteristics: Following appositive contains its own modifiers, requiring single comma not paired dashes
Distractors include colon and dash variants
medium.example_stem: ...one such type of matter is a form of cooled, condensed gas called a Bose-Einstein condensate ______ Dutch physicist Lene Hau famously used a BEC...

characteristics: Choice between dash and colon for emphasis at a list-introducing position
Stimulus introduces a uniquely identified concept ('the chemical compound', 'two varieties of the sweet potato plant') followed by named instances
Correct answer often a colon or dash, not a comma
hard.example_stem: ...examined the defensive responses of two varieties of the sweet potato ______ TN57, which is known for its insect resistance, and TN66...

must_preserve: The descriptor must be definite or possessive (the/her/his/this/that)
The name must follow the descriptor and be the only instance referenced
Correct answer must set the name off with comma, dash, or colon depending on position
must_vary: Type of descriptor (work, person, concept)
Position relative to sentence end
common_bugs: Cloner drops the definite article, demoting to T6 restrictive
Cloner places the descriptor after the name, reversing the structure
Cloner mismatches: uses 'a' but keeps comma punctuation, conflicting with the rule

aspects.name: Items in a series or list
aspects.description: The student must punctuate a sequence of three or more parallel items. The cognitive operation is determining whether items are simple (use commas) or themselves contain internal commas (use semicolons), and whether the sequence is preceded by a colon-worthy introductory clause.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 always involves three or more parallel elements; A2's appositive is a single insertion. Reducing a three-item list to a single appositive is a categorical drift.

types.name: Comma-separated parallel series
types.description: Three or more short, comma-free items are listed; the student selects the comma after the item before the blank and rejects colons, semicolons, or omitted commas.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the items as parallel simple elements and apply comma separators consistently across the series.

characteristics: All items are single nouns or short noun phrases
List position in the stem is unambiguous
Distractors include colon, semicolon, dash
easy.example_stem: ...enforcing a moral code... which established the sanctity of animal ______ the just treatment of the elderly, and the abolition of the slave trade.

characteristics: Items are verb phrases or longer parallel constituents
Stem has a wider context that pushes attention away from the list
Distractors include comma + extra word ('and')
medium.example_stem: More advanced floats can measure a wider range of ______ and monitor seasonal ice zones.

characteristics: Items contain longer descriptive phrases; commas inside might be mistaken for series separators
Distractors include semicolons that would be valid if items had internal commas
hard.example_stem: ...divergent strategies for preserving milk ______ whereas rennet-coagulated cheesemaking became key... acid-heat coagulation methods became common...

must_preserve: Three or more parallel items in the series
Each item must be free of internal commas
Correct answer must be a simple comma
must_vary: Number of items (3 vs 4)
Grammatical category of items (nouns, verbs, infinitives)
common_bugs: Cloner adds a comma inside one item, breaking the simple-list condition (T9 drift)
Cloner reduces to two items, eliminating the series
Cloner introduces colon-style introducer, demanding T10 instead

types.name: Semicolon-separated complex series
types.description: Three or more items each containing internal commas (parenthetical, dates, or descriptive phrases) require semicolons as the outer separators. The student selects semicolon at the blank to maintain parallel separation.
types.core_skill_demand: Detect that at least one item in the series contains an internal comma and therefore mandates semicolon separators across all items.

characteristics: (rare at this difficulty in the bank)
Two of three items contain internal commas; remaining option mixes semicolon/comma to test consistency
medium.example_stem: ...awarded to three pioneers...: two-time Nobel laureate Barry Sharpless, who coined the term...; Carolyn Bertozzi, founder of the Bertozzi Group at ______ and Morten Meldal, a professor...

characteristics: All items have internal modifiers (dates, parenthetical descriptions, appositives)
Distractors mix semicolon-comma-comma orderings to force full-series consistency check
Stem often spans many words across two sentences, increasing parsing load
hard.example_stem: ...includes The Joys of Motherhood, a novel about the changing roles of women in 1950s ______ A Kind of Marriage, a television play...; and Head Above Water, her autobiography.

must_preserve: At least one item in the series must contain an internal comma
Outer separator must be semicolon at the blank
Series must have three or more items
must_vary: Type of internal modifier (date, descriptor, parenthetical)
Number of items beyond three
common_bugs: Cloner removes internal commas, removing the justification for semicolons
Cloner makes only one item complex, but accepts mixed comma/semicolon answer
Cloner shortens to two items, eliminating series structure entirely

types.name: Colon or dash introducing a list/elaboration
types.description: An independent clause introduces a list, definition, or elaboration that follows the blank. The student selects a colon (or sometimes a dash) to mark the introduction; commas and semicolons are rejected.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that the material before the blank is a complete sentence that promises an elaboration, and the material after is a list or single elaborating phrase, then select colon (or dash for emphatic break).

characteristics: (rare at easy; bank shows colon-list mostly at medium/hard)
Pre-blank clause is short and clearly complete; post-blank is a named instance or short list
Distractors include period, semicolon, and comma
medium.example_stem: The conference's goals were ______ ensure women's participation in the proceedings... to secure the right of women to serve... and to advocate for human rights.

characteristics: Pre-blank clause itself contains an embedded modifier or appositive
Post-blank elaboration is a single named instance or a longer descriptive explanation
Distractors include dashes and semicolons that mimic the rhythm but are technically wrong
hard.example_stem: Quantum particles... an unhackable means of transmitting encryption keys... attempts to observe particles will alter the particles ______ dismantle any information they transmit.

must_preserve: Material before blank must be a grammatically complete sentence
Material after blank must function as a list, definition, or elaboration of something promised before
Correct answer must be colon (or dash) — not comma or semicolon
must_vary: Whether the elaboration is a single phrase or a multi-item list
Topic and named referents
common_bugs: Cloner makes the pre-blank a fragment, breaking the colon-requires-complete-sentence rule
Cloner adds 'such as' before blank, removing colon-licensing need
Cloner provides a coordinator after the blank, demoting to T1 or T8

aspects.name: End-of-sentence punctuation and question vs statement
aspects.description: The student must choose the terminating punctuation and word order at the end of a sentence: period for declarative, question mark for direct question. The cognitive operation is determining whether the sentence reports an indirect question (declarative, period, subject-then-verb) or asks a direct question (interrogative, question mark, verb-then-subject inversion).
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 governs only sentence-final boundary plus subject-verb order in question/statement contrast; other aspects govern internal punctuation. A clone that swaps a comma question for a sentence-final question is a categorical drift.

types.name: Direct question vs indirect/embedded question
types.description: The blank ends a sentence that may be either a reported indirect question (declarative, period) or a direct quoted-style question (interrogative, '?'). Word order (subject-verb vs verb-subject inversion) and the framing verb determine which is correct.
types.core_skill_demand: Detect whether the sentence frames the question as something being asked directly (use inversion and '?') or as something embedded inside a reporting clause (use normal order and '.').

characteristics: Sentence opens with 'How', 'Where', 'Why', or 'Which' as direct question; or with 'Researchers investigated how/whether' as indirect
Four options differ only on '.'/' ?' and S-V order
Context paragraph cues which framing the sentence intends
easy.example_stem: How ______ A layer of microbubbles on their plumage reduces friction as the penguins speed to the surface.

characteristics: (bank shows this type concentrated at Easy; no medium originals present)
(bank shows this type concentrated at Easy; no hard originals present)

must_preserve: The framing word before the blank must license either direct (How/Where/Why with no prior reporting verb) or indirect (X investigated how/Y wondered whether) reading, and the keyed answer matches
Four options must span the 2x2 of period/question mark × normal/inverted word order
must_vary: Question word (How/Where/Why/Which/Whether)
Topic of the underlying question
common_bugs: Cloner replaces the framing verb so both indirect and direct readings become possible, ambiguating the key
Cloner inverts only one option's word order, breaking the 2x2 distractor pattern
Cloner places the blank mid-sentence rather than at terminal position, demoting to A1/A3

skill_wide_notes: Across all 189 originals, every stem is the canonical 'Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?' MCQ with four punctuation/word-order variants. Difficulty rises (a) from single-issue choices at Easy (period vs comma, restrictive vs nonrestrictive name) to (b) compound issues at Hard where the four options vary on multiple axes (e.g., '; however,' vs ', however;' vs ', however,' vs ', however'). Hard items disproportionately combine A1+A3 (clause boundary involving named expert) or A4 T10 (colon-introduced elaboration); easy items skew to A1 T1/T2 (clean clause joins) and A5 (direct/indirect question). Cloners must check that exactly one option satisfies all conventions simultaneously and that distractors each fail on a single identifiable rule.
